data.title: Is Iron Mountain Still Attractive After a 25.9% One Year Slide?
data.description:
• Wondering if Iron Mountain is still worth digging into after its big run over the past few years, or if the value story has already played out? Here is a breakdown of whether the current price really matches the long term fundamentals.
  • After a 249.9% gain over 5 years and 73.3% over 3 years, the stock has cooled off with a 17.7% drop over the last 30 days and is now down 18.8% year to date and 25.9% over the past year, including a 1.4% decline in just the last week.
  • That pullback has come as investors reassess high flying yield and infrastructure style names more broadly and rotate toward other income and AI themed plays. This is happening even though Iron Mountain continues to position itself as a key player in data centers and information management. Headlines have focused on its strategic moves into digital infrastructure and long term customer contracts, which help explain both the enthusiasm that drove past gains and the more cautious tone recently.
  • Despite the recent volatility, Iron Mountain currently scores a solid 5/6 on our valuation checks, suggesting it still appears undervalued on most of the metrics we track. Next, we walk through the main valuation approaches behind that score, and later explore an additional way to think about what this business may be worth.

Find out why Iron Mountain's -25.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Iron Mountain Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The DCF model estimates what Iron Mountain is worth today by projecting its adjusted funds from operations into the future and discounting those cash flows back to a present value. For Iron Mountain, this approach starts with last twelve months free cash flow of about $1.34 billion, then applies analyst forecasts for the next few years and extends those trends further out.

Analysts expect free cash flow to rise steadily, with projections reaching roughly $3.07 billion by 2029. Beyond the explicit analyst horizon, Simply Wall St extrapolates growth so that by 2035, projected free cash flow is just over $5.07 billion. All of these figures are in $ and are discounted back using a 2 stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model based on adjusted funds from operations.

Adding up those discounted cash flows results in an estimated intrinsic value of about $217.19 per share. This implies the stock is trading at roughly a 60.8% discount to this fair value estimate. On this basis, Iron Mountain appears materially undervalued according to the DCF approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Iron Mountain Price vs Sales

For profitable, revenue generating businesses like Iron Mountain, the price to sales ratio is a useful way to compare how much investors are paying for each dollar of revenue across different companies and over time. A higher multiple can be justified when a company has stronger growth prospects or lower perceived risk, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower, more conservative multiple.

Iron Mountain currently trades on a price to sales ratio of about 3.79x, which is below both the Specialized REITs industry average of roughly 7.12x and the peer group average of around 6.69x. Simply Wall St’s proprietary Fair Ratio, which estimates what a reasonable price to sales multiple should be after accounting for Iron Mountain’s growth outlook, risk profile, profit margins, industry and market cap, comes in at 5.18x.

This Fair Ratio is more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for Iron Mountain’s specific fundamentals rather than assuming all REITs deserve the same multiple. With the current 3.79x sitting well below the 5.18x Fair Ratio, the price to sales view also points to Iron Mountain potentially being undervalued.

data.title: Aurubis (XTRA:NDA) Margin Squeeze and EPS Collapse Challenge Long-Term Bullish Narrative
data.description:
Aurubis (XTRA:NDA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €4.4 billion alongside EPS of €1.04, setting the tone for a year where profit margins stayed under pressure but remained positive. Over the past six quarters, the company has seen revenue move from €4.2 billion in Q4 2024 to a range between roughly €4.2 billion and €5.0 billion per quarter, while quarterly EPS has swung between €1.04 and €5.87, highlighting how sensitive earnings are to underlying margin shifts. With trailing 12 month net profit margin sitting at 1.4% versus 2.4% a year earlier, investors are likely to read this update as a reminder that the near term story hinges less on sales volume and more on whether margins can stabilize.

See our full analysis for Aurubis.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this margin story lines up with the dominant market narratives around Aurubis and where those stories may need an update.

See what the community is saying about Aurubis

Advertisement

Margins Squeeze Net Income To €261 Million TTM

  • Over the last twelve months, Aurubis generated €261 million of net income on €18.2 billion of revenue, which translates into a 1.4% net margin versus 2.4% a year earlier.
  • Bears argue that margin compression will be a structural drag, and the recent data give them some support:
    • Trailing net income fell from €416 million a year ago to €261 million now, while revenue rose from €17.2 billion to €18.2 billion, so more sales are translating into less profit.
    • Five year earnings have declined around 5.9% per year even before considering forecasts for margins to shrink from 3.4% to 1.3%, which aligns with concerns about slower profit growth than topline expansion.
In a market where small shifts in margin can make or break the thesis, skeptics see the drop from €416 million to €261 million of trailing net income as a warning sign that recent profitability may not justify a premium narrative. 🐻 Aurubis Bear Case

EPS Slide From €5.87 To €1.04 Tests Growth Hopes

  • Within FY 2025, quarterly EPS moved from €5.87 in Q1 to €3.73 in Q2, €1.78 in Q3 and just €1.04 in Q4, even as revenue stayed between about €4.2 billion and €5.0 billion each quarter.
  • Consensus narrative points to strong long term copper demand and strategic projects as growth drivers, yet the EPS path adds nuance:
    • TTM EPS has dropped from €15.9 at Q2 2025 to €6.0 by Q4 2025, while analysts still expect earnings to grow about 11% per year, so the base they are growing from has already been reset lower.
    • Projects like the Richmond recycling facility are expected to add around €170 million of EBITDA within a wider €260 million strategic uplift, but near term ramp up and cost pressure are showing up as weaker per share profitability today.

DCF Shows 33% Upside Despite 1.4% Margin

  • At a share price of €119.10, Aurubis trades about 33% below a DCF fair value of €178.35, while its P E of 19.9 times sits below the 63.8 times peer average yet above the 16.2 times European Metals and Mining industry level.
  • Bullish investors lean on this valuation gap and future projects, and the current numbers partly back that stance:
    • Even with net margin at 1.4% and five year earnings down 5.9% annually, analysts still project roughly 4% annual revenue growth and around 11% annual earnings growth, suggesting the current earnings trough may not be permanent.
    • Forecast earnings of €270.2 million and EPS of €6.11 by around 2028, combined with an assumed future P E of 14.8 times, imply that if Aurubis reaches those levels from today’s softer base, the present discount to DCF could offer meaningful upside.
Bulls see a stock priced at €119.10 with a DCF fair value of €178.35 and medium term earnings growth forecasts as a potential mispricing worth digging into before margins turn. 🐂 Aurubis Bull Case
